name: Truckee Tavern
description: We believe a great dining experience must not only be made with fresh quality ingredients and be expertly prepared but it must invoke emotion and memory. Truckee Tavern’s food and beverage offerings include the freshest, highest quality local ingredients, farm raised, hormone free meats and locally grown produce to give a sense of place. Menu items showcase the incredible diversity of food available in California. The menus changes continually to reflect the seasons, changing tastes and availability to give the feel of moment. Retaining crowd favorites with new and exciting interpretations invokes anticipation in our regulars. These offerings are paired expertly with the finest wine, spirit and beer selections. Truckee Tavern’s spirit program draws from Truckee’s rich history of bootlegging during prohibition and focus on artisanal small batch gin and whiskey, the two main distillation products from Truckee during that era. This is complemented by a thoughtfully chosen selection of fine rum, vodka, brandy, tequila, mescal, liquors and cordials, all complimenting the overall experience.

name: Wild Cherries Coffee House
description: Wild Cherries Coffee House has been serving the community since 1998. Kristin Krone, a native Truckee resident and two time Olympian in alpine skiing, along with Gary Talbot, local entrepreneur and fellow coffee connoisseur became acquainted in 1984 at the first local coffee Roastery, Truckee River Coffee Roasters. A shared passion for excellent fresh roasted coffees, European style pastries and a place to meet friends eventually led to a business partnership. In 1995 they began their own coffee roasting venture which ultimately led to Wild Cherries Coffee House in 1998. In 2003 Kristin became sole owner of Wild Cherries continuing the tradition she and Gary had created. The inspiration and mission of Wild Cherries has always been to create an establishment where everyone feels welcome, providing excellent locally roasted coffees and fresh homemade delicious fare. Over 20 years later Wild Cherries continues to be the place to be, a locale for all, where our baristas make you smile, our bustling kitchen makes you happy and the people you meet here will make your day.

name: Music in the Park Truckee

description: The Indian Springs Art & Ceramic Center is a project art space and gallery devoted to the promotion of the ceramic arts in Nevada County and California. Our facility offers knowledgeable staff, scheduled juried exhibits, artist workshops and instructional classes with discussion on "making" clay objects and firing technologies used. name: Alvin Stevens Trivelpiece
description: Alvin Stevens Trivelpiece 1903-1967. Californian, Clamper, Nevada County Historian, Newspaperman, Friend and a Founding Father of the Malakoff State Park, in whose memory these benches were placed.

name: Anthony House Site
description: Anthony House and Ranch. In 1849, a Mr. Anthony staked out 160 acres on the north side of Deer Creek on the road which would become part of the Marysville-Henness Pass-Virginia City trade route, and built a house and out-buildings. While further details about Mr. Anthony are unknown, the name Anthony House prevailed through a succession of owners. The property was up for sale in 1852 and purchased by Mr. S. P. French. By that time it was a flourishing stage stop, serving as a hotel, restaurant, livery stable and post office. The original house burned down in 1876 but was quickly rebuilt. Subsequent owners included William Henry Davey, Nevada County Supervisor 1908-1916 and later his son, Frank Davey. Stage stop and post office operations continued to 1906 when the post office was moved to Smartsville. Thereafter, declining traffic on the trade route led to farming and cattle raising as the main use of the property. By 1970 when Boise Cascade began development of this area, the property had fallen into disrepair and the house was burned down as a training exercise by the Penn Valley Fire Protection District. Construction of Anthony Dam here on Deer Creek submerged any remains of this historic site under Lake Wildwood.
address: East side of Pleasant Valley Rd next to dam spillway at Lake Wildwood

image.alt: Anthony House Site, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Blue Tent
description: Blue Tent is a historic mining community established in 1850 and named after its first dwelling, a tent made of blue denim. In its heyday, it had a population of over 350, and its own store, dancehall, blacksmith shop, boarding house and other buildings. A non-denominational Union Church was erected in 1861, and a school was established in 1868. A substantial Chinatown existed just north of Blue Tent. The community was connected by stage to Nevada City, and by the world's first long distance telephone line established in 1878, to the mining communities around the San Juan Ridge. A post office existed between 1878 and 1889. In addition to mining, Blue Tent for many years had a thriving industry harvesting ice and shipping it throughout California. Like many communities that depended on hydraulic mining, Blue Tent began to decline following the 1884 Sawyer decision, forbidding the discharge of mining debris into the Yuba River. As mining declined, agriculture began to predominate. Today, the Blue Tent district is a pleasant, rural, largely residential area. Not much evidence remains of the mining district, except the Blue Tent school, which is still at the site to which it was moved in 1892. It has been converted to a private residence, but remains painted a vivid blue.
address: About 5 miles northeast of Nevada City, approximately at the intersection of N. Bloomfield and Blue Tent School Rds

image.alt: Blue Tent, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Carleton Watkins, via Wikimedia Commons (Public domain)

name: Boca Brewery
description: Boca. Noted as a railroad, ice and lumbering center since 1867 but most fondly remembered and hereby honored as home of the Boca Brewery which was located 1/2 mile south of this point across the Truckee River. Boca Lager Beer was world famous and mercifully slaked the thirst of a grateful mankind. Second plaque placed 2001 by Chap 3691 E Clampus Vitus: Boca Beer. Warranted to keep in any weather.
address: East end of Boca Dam, 1 m. north of 1-80 on Stampede Rd, about 5 m. east of Truckee

image.alt: Boca Brewery, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Boca Dam - Newlands Reclamation Project
description: Designated a National Historic Landmark 1981: Boca Dam 1937-1940. United States Department of the Interior, Harold L. Ickes, Secretary; Bureau of Reclamation, John C. Page, Commissioner; R. F. Walter, Chief Engineer; L. J. Foster and C. S. Hale, Construction Engineers. Height 116 feet, Length 1629 feet, Storage capacity 40,901 acre-feet. Contractor, George W. Condon Co. Note: Originally called the Truckee-Carson Project, the Newlands Project covers a large area in western Nevada and a smaller area of Truckee-Tahoe area. One of five authorized by the Federal Reclamation Act of 1902, to reclaim arid lands for agricultural purposes. Renamed in 1919 in honor of U.S. Senator Francis G. Newlands of Nevada, water from natural flow and storage in Lake Tahoe and Boca Reservoir is diverted from Truckee River into the 32.5-mile Truckee Canal and then discharged into the Carson River.
address: East end of Boca Dam, Stampede Road 1 m. north of I-80, about 5 m. east of Truckee

image.alt: Boca Dam - Newlands Reclamation Project, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Boca Ice Harvest Site
description: From 1868 to 1927 huge quantities of ice were harvested from the Truckee Basin. This provided employment and significantly contributed to the area's economic growth and its colorful history. Boca Pond was one of the first to be used to harvest ice and ship it across the nation. It was one of the last of the ice plants to survive competition from artificial ice companies.
address: Stampede Rd, 2 m. north of 1-80, 5 m. east of Truckee

image.alt: Boca Ice Harvest Site, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Boca Ice & Fine Arts Center, via Google

name: Bridgeport Covered Bridge
description: This covered bridge across the South Fork of the Yuba River at Bridgeport, Nevada County. Built in 1862 by David Isaac John Wood with lumber from his mill in Sierra County, this bridge was part of the Virginia Turnpike Company Toll Road which served the Northern Mines and the busy Nevada Comstock Lode. Utilizing a combination truss and arch construction, it is one of the oldest housed spans in the West and the longest single span, wooden covered bridge in the United States.The plaque is dedicated to those pioneer Americans who came to California in search of a new life and passed this way to establish their homes in the Northern Mine country. Note: The second plaque is full of errors. Bridgeport originally was known as "Nye's Landing" because Urias and Manuel Nye built a trading post here in 1849, but "Nye's Crossing" was on the Middle Fork of the Yuba River, near the mouth of Oregon Creek. Furthermore, the builder's name was David Ingerfield John Wood.
address: Pleasant Valley Rd at South Fork of Yuba River

image.alt: Bridgeport Covered Bridge, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Frank Schulenburg, via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 4.0)

name: Brimskill Building
description: Built by Edward Brimskill, 1849-1851. Rear section built 1849. Iron shutters a protection from fire and robbers, imported from Scotland. Used in early days as general store and miners supply. Wells Fargo maintained desk room here for many years and shipped thousands of dollars of gold from here. One of the best preserved buildings in California of the gold rush era. Said to be oldest building in Nevada County.

image.alt: Brimskill Building, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: CA Historical Marker- Floriston (Bronco)
description: Established in 1872 as Bronco, this railroad wood stop became a noted ice and lumber center and was renamed in 1891. The Floriston Pulp and Paper Company built the present town after 1899 and operated one of the world's largest pulp mill through 1930. It paper products and ice helped California's agricultural industry feed the hungry of the world.

image.alt: CA Historical Marker- Floriston (Bronco) in Floriston

name: Canyon Creek Bridge Site
description: The Canyon Creek Bridge was built in 1895 by Cotton Brothers and Co. of Oakland, California, for $1,725. Completed two years earlier than the Old Auburn Bridge (No. 13,), it is of similar construction and design. Underneath it is the reservoir that supplies water to the residents of the town of Washington. Canyon Creek is a major tributary of the South Yuba River, and the scenic view of the rapids and falls above the bridge is outstanding. Note: After determining the bridge had outlived its useful life, the County erected a modern bridge in 2017 and placed this plaque: Site of a steel pony truss bridge erected in 1895 at Canyon Creek Crossing on the road linking Washington to the town of Maybert. There the Yuba Mine employed 13 Chinese and 27 Americans, one of the few mines still employing Chinese. In the 1880's and 1890's Maybert had a Post Office, store, boarding house, school and saloon. Fire destroyed all 20 of its dwellings in 1909. The old bridge was disassembled and is awaiting relocation by Nevada City as part of its hiking trails expansion.
address: Maybert Rd at Canyon Creek, 3.3 m. east of town of Washington. Maybert Rd does not require four-wheel-drive but is not advised for cars with low clearance

image.alt: Canyon Creek Bridge Site, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: National Geodetic Survey NGS. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), National Ocean Service (NOS) office., via Wikimedia Commons (Public domain)

name: Cherokee
description: Cherokee is a former gold mining community In Nevada County, located on the San Juan Ridge about 4 miles east of North San Juan. At the time of the Gold Rush, this area was occupied by the Nisenan. The Gold Rush came to Cherokee in 1850, when a group of Cherokee Indians began mining there and gave it its name. The town's heyday was between 1856 and 1865. It had two hotels, the Grizzly and Curnow's, saloons, several stores, a butcher, a shoe shop, a resident physician and drugstore, and a blacksmith. Its sawmill had a capacity of 10,000 ft. of lumber a day. The local newspaper was the Cherokee Journal. There was stagecoach service to Nevada City. After 1878, Cherokee was connected to the world's first long distance telephone line which connected the mining communities along the San Juan Ridge from French Corral to the Bowman Lake area. The town began to decline in the 1880's, after hydraulic mining was restricted by the Sawyer decision. By 1924, the town was reduced to "one store; and the people live principally by farming, very little active mining being carried on here." Today, all that remains of Cherokee is the schoolhouse located on Sages Road just north of Tyler Foote Crossing Road, the historic cemetery, and a lot of land scarred by hydraulic mining.
address: North of Nevada City on Tyler Foote Rd, about 5 miles east of Hwy 49, just past Sages Rd

image.alt: Cherokee, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Alexander Migl, via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 4.0)

name: Chicago Park Historic Agricultural Community
description: The Chicago Park Historic Agricultural Community occupies roughly the area on either side of Highway 174 between the Bear River and Brunswick Road. It is centered on the villages of Chicago Park and Peardale. Prior to the 1840s, this area was populated by the Nisenan. In August 1849, what is reported to have been Nevada County's second store was erected on land overlooking the junction of the Bear River and Greenhorn Creek. In the 1850s, local residents led by Simmon Storms succeeded in having the Nisenan removed to a reservation near Tehama. During that period, Benjamin Taylor settled on a ranch in the area which he named Buena Vista, after the Mexican War battle in which he had participated, and engaged in farming and horse breeding. Others who settled in the area found it very suitable for growing fruit, especially pears. In 1872, the Coleman Brothers purchased the Buena Vista and Storms ranches, and other properties in the area, as they were developing the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. Completed in 1876, the Railroad spurred the agricultural development of the area. In 1887, a group of Colfax and Chicago, Illinois developers acquired several thousand acres of land and drew up plans for the Chicago Park Colony. The Colony was to consist of 20 and 40 acre tracts as well as town lots laid out around a public square with streets named for prominent streets in Chicago. Much of the planned development never materialized, but reportedly almost 7000 acres were sold, principally to residents of Chicago. Many of the tracts were planted with orchards, and the area was referred to as the Bartlett Pear Belt. The railroad station at Storms Station was renamed Chicago Park Station, There were also stations at Buena Vista and Peardale. Fruit was king in the area for about 100-years. Orchards growing pears, plums, peaches and apples were everywhere. Because water was not abundant, dry farming techniques were mastered which improved fruit color. In 1915, local Bartlett pears were honored at the Pacific International Exposition in San Francisco. As the 20th century progressed, pear blight appeared, causing some growers to switch to other crops.
address: Along Hwy 174 between the Bear River and Brunswick Rd

image.alt: Chicago Park Historic Agricultural Community, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Chief Truckee
description: Chief Truckee. The legendary Paiute leader; friend and guide to pioneers; breveted a 'Captain' by John C. Fremont; gave his name to this valley, river and town; died near Dayton, Nevada, in 1860.
address: Truckee River Regional Park, Hwy. 267, 1 m. south of Truckee

image.alt: Chief Truckee, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: China Wall
description: Third plaque: The China Wall. In the 1850s, Chinese laborers, who were an essential part of the community, built the wall you see before you. Large natural stones were hand stacked for its construction. The wall extends 5 miles in either direction, however much of it has disintegrated over the years. This lot, along with the lot across the Gephardt's Union Turnpike, and the adjoining lots, are referred to in the Nevada County tax records as the Ã¯China LotsÃ

image.alt: China Wall, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: China Wall at Donner Summit
description: Charles Crocker, construction chief of the Central Pacific Railroad (CPRR), contracted for a workforce of approximately 12,000 Chinese laborers to push the CPRR tracks over its Trans-Sierra crossing on its race east to meet with the Union Pacific at Promontory, Utah Territory. A railroad retaining wall and fill, constructed of Sierra granite, stand silently above on the pass as a lasting monument to these Asian 'Master Builders' who left an indelible mark on the history of California and the West.
address: South side of Donner Pass Rd (Old Hwy 40), west of Donner Lake about 0.5 m. below summit

image.alt: China Wall at Donner Summit, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Clinton Narrow Gauge Railroad
description: First steam railroad logging operation in the Truckee River Basin. Commenced operation in the late 1870's under the supervision of Fred Burckhalter, head of the Pacific Lumber and Wood Co. Line extended 10 miles along Juniper Creek with hope of eventual service to Lake Tahoe. Line abandoned in 1901 after several conflagrations at the PL&W Co. mill in Clinton.
address: Southeast corner of Hirschdale Rd, and Juniper Way, about 5 m. east of Truckee and 1 m. south of I-80

image.alt: Clinton Narrow Gauge Railroad, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Columbia Hill School
description: The schoolhouse, built in 1875, was still in use as a school in the 1970's. In 1980 the State of California condemned the schoolhouse as unsafe. It was remodeled and used for a while as a community center. Later a new community center was established next door, and the old school building was used for other purposes. It is now known as the North Columbia Schoolhouse Cultural Center. The original Columbia Hill School was built prior to March 11,1867.
address: Tyler Foote Rd, San Juan Ridge, 8.7 m. east of Hwy 49, northeast of Nevada City

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Columbia Hill School
image.originalAlt: Columbia Hill School in

name: Commercial Row / Brickelltown Historic District
description: The Commercial Row/ Brickelltown Historic District lies in the center of Truckee along the tracks of the first transcontinental railroad. It includes the first built areas of Truckee. Because of fires, most of the buildings in the district range from 1870 to the 1920s. The district illustrates Truckee's relationship with the railroad and the lumber industry and Truckee's appearance more than 100-years ago. Truckee was originally called Coburn Station, and then renamed around 1868 after a Paiute chief. The District was dedicated in 2009 by the Truckee Town Council.

image.alt: Commercial Row / Brickelltown Historic District, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Davis Mill
description: The Davis Mill is a well preserved stamp mill which served a small gold mine run by the Davis family from 1915 to 1940.
address: Off North Bloomfield Rd; 3 m. NE of Nevada City. Not open to the public

image.alt: Davis Mill

name: Donner Camp at Alder Creek Valley
description: This site has three plaques and was designated a National Historical Landmark in 1966. First plaque dedicated 1996 by Tahoe National Forest: In the fall of 1846, 25 members of the Donner Party became trapped by an early snowstorm here at Alder Creek Valley. The George and Jacob Donner families, their teamsters, and fellow travelers suffered extreme hardship and starvation. They spent the winter here cut off from the rest of their party who camped at Donner Lake. Only eleven survived the ordeal. Their survival, against desperate odds, stands as a testament to the enduring pioneer spirit that helped shape the future of California. Second plaque dedicated 1996 by Chap 3691 E Clampus Vitus: They gave unselfishly, their fortunes and their lives that their children should survive. Near this site, in the winter of 1846, two pioneer women gave up their lives for their families. Tamsen and Elizabeth Donner feared their many children could not survive the ravages of cold and starvation when the party was caught in an early blizzard. They provided care and comfort to their families and companions throughout the snowbound winter, desperately trying to prevent the death of their loved ones. Both lost their lives. However, most of their children survived to carry their mothers' dreams of new life and new beginnings to the valleys of California. The summit that they never crossed now bears the family name. An inspiration to all who followed their footsteps across the Sierra Nevada Mountains, we herein honor the memory and the sacrifices of these women. Their struggle to survive, enduring hardships we can barely imagine, remains a legacy of the pioneer spirit. On this 150th anniversary of their tragic encampment the Chief Truckee Chapter of E Clampus Vitus dedicates this monument to their spirit and the preservation of their memory that we not forget the sacrifices they made in opening California to its destiny. The Donner Camp Site plaque is located in the middle of the Donner Camp Interpretive Trail, just past the end of the boardwalk. Second plaque dedicated 1960 by Chap 10 E Clampus Vitus, Historical Society, Tahoe National Forest USFS, Truckee-Donner Chamber of Commerce, and Native Daughters of the Golden West: On October 28, 1846 the six covered wagons brought west by George and Jacob Donner and their families halted here for repairs. By March of 1847 one half of the party of 22 adults and children had died of starvation and cold. They came west seeking a new life and found misery and death.
address: Alder Creek Picnic Site; 2.75 m. north of 1-80 on Hwy 89, 0.1 m. east of Hwy 89, Truckee

image.alt: Donner Camp at Alder Creek Valley, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Donner Emigrant Trail
description: The Emigrant Trail in the Pioneer Days of California came through the low pass to the north, facing this monument. The Trail turned west at this point for a distance of twenty-six hundred feet where a tablet describes the route then followed. Adjoining plaque dedicated 2014: First Permanent Emigrant Trail Markers. The nearby monument was envisioned in 1929 by C.F. McGlashan, Truckee's foremost resident 1872-1931 and author of the 1880 classic, The History of the Donner Party; P.M. Weddell, who placed wooden signs on the Donner Trail from Verdi, Nevada over Coldstream Pass to Lake Mary from 1924 until his death in 1951; and W.F. Knowland, U. S. Senator (1945-1959) and the chairman of The Native Sons of the Golden West's Landmark Commission. The three trail authorities met in McGlashan's law office in Truckee to draw up the text for the plaque on this monument, listed as #3 on the 1930 Weddell map to the right and a companion plaque for the monument listed as #5 located one half mile to the west on old Hwy 40 and Coldstream Road. Note: According to the P. M. Weddell map of the Historic Emigrant Road, this resting place of the Native Sons' marker is close to its original location.
address: Front of Tahoe Truckee High School, 11725 Donner Pass Rd, Truckee

image.alt: Donner Emigrant Trail, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Ken Lund from Reno, Nevada, USA, via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 2.0)

name: Donner Party - Graves Cabin Site
description: Plaque dedicated 1919 by Native Sons of the Golden West: Donner Party 1846-1847. On this spot stood the Graves Cabin.
address: Opposite 12020 Donner Pass Rd in West Truckee

image.alt: Donner Party - Graves Cabin Site, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Donner Summit Bridge
description: First plaque dedicated 1926 by Native Sons of the Golden West: Dedicated to the pioneers who blazed the Overland Trail through these mountains. Built by the U. S. Bureau of Public Roads and the California Highway Commission, 1925-1926. Landmark registered and second plaque dedicated 1998 by Nevada County: 1926 Donner Summit Bridge Restored 1995. Note: In 1926 it was anticipated that the bridge (built at a cost of about $40,000) would become a popular stopping place on the "Victory Highway." The bridge was designed by Norman C. Raab, whose wife, Ruby Bishop Raab, was the great-granddaughter of Isaac Hitchcock of the Stephens-Townsend-Murphy party. The original bridge arch was 110 feet long and 70 feet high, built on a compound curve, with a seven percent grade. Among those who attended the 1926 ceremony were Charles F. McGlashan and these descendants of Donner party survivors: James Fraser Lewis, Martha Jane Lewis, Susan A. Lewis, and Ernest H. Murphy. Norman Raab also designed the world-famous Bixby Creek Bridge on State Highway 1 at Big Sur, built in 1930-1931.
address: Donner Pass Rd (Old Hwy 40) overlooking Donner Lake

image.alt: Donner Summit Bridge, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Ken Lund, via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 2.0)

name: Donner Summit Tunnel
description: "Great Summit Tunnel of the Sierra Nevada. Beneath this plaque the first Transcontinental Railroad traversed the mighty Sierra Nevada range. The 1659 foot Long summit tunnel took over 15 months of Chinese muscle and sweat to build. The Chinese painstakingly hand drilled, then blasted the granite rock with black powder and newly invented nitroglycerine. A vertical shaft took 85 days to complete and allowed tunnel construction to proceed from the center as well as from the portals. The most difficult obstacle facing the Central Pacific Railroad was overcome when the tunnel was completed in August 1867. The first passenger train passed through the tunnel on June 18, 1868. The last train went through in 1993."
address: On Donner Pass Rd, about 3.8 mi. south of I-80, Truckee. Turn at Donner Summit Elevation 7,135 ft. marker. In the Lamson-Cash Donner Summit Hub across parking lot from Summit Haus.

image.alt: Donner Summit Tunnel

name: Donner or Pioneer Monument
description: Monument dedicated and first two plaques placed 1918 by the Governors of California and Nevada, three survivors of the Donner Party, and Native Daughters of the Golden West. First plaque: Virile to risk and find, Kindly withal and a ready help. Facing the brunt of fate. Indomitable-unafraid." Second plaque: In commemoration of the pioneers who crossed the plains to settle in California. Landmark registered and third plaque placed 1962 by State of California, CHL 134: Donner Party. Near this spot stood the Breen Cabin of the party of emigrants who started for California from Springfield, Illinois, in April, 1846, under the leadership of Captain George Donner. Delays occurred and when the party reached this locality, on October 29, the Truckee Pass emigrant road was concealed by snow. The height of the shaft of the monument indicates the depth of the snow, which was twenty-two feet. After futile efforts to cross the summit the party was compelled to encamp for the winter. The Graves cabin was situated about three- quarters of a mile to the eastward, the Murphy cabin was about two hundred yards southwest of the monument, and the Donner tents were at the head of Alder Creek. Ninety people were in the party and forty-two perished, most of them from starvation and exposure.
address: In Donner Memorial State Park, about 100 yds. east of Museum

image.alt: Donner or Pioneer Monument, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Dr. W. C. Jones Home Site
description: Dr. W. C. Jones came to Grass Valley in 1874 after serving as physician and surgeon for the Central Pacific Railroad Co. during construction of the railroad between Colfax and Reno. He had three sons, Will, John, and Carl, who along with their father served the community as doctors until the last son died in 1944. In 1904 Dr. John Jones opened the first private hospital in Nevada County-the W. C. Jones Memorial Hospital, Grass Valley. A fourth son, George, became Superior Court Judge of Nevada County, serving from 1908 until 1925 and again from 1938 until he retired in 1947. Note: The city removed the historic house to make room for the police department.
address: 129 S Auburn St, now Grass Valley Police Dept

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Dr. W. C. Jones Home Site
image.originalAlt: Dr. W. C. Jones Home Site in

name: Edwards Crossing Bridge
description: Built in 1904 by the American Bridge Company of steel-connection-pin design to replace a wooden structure built in 1862 by William Edwards. In 1854 Edwards began operations here with a ferry, and in 1855 he built a bridge and toll road between Blue Tent and Lake City. His first bridge washed away in December 1861, and was replaced in the following year. The county acquired the bridge sometime after 1880. Note: Both this and the similar Gault bridge at Nevada City (No. 99) were built under the supervision of Nevada County Surveyor W. W. Waggoner.
address: On North Bloomfield Rd at South Fork of Yuba River, northeast of Nevada City

image.alt: Edwards Crossing Bridge, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Emigrant Trail on Coldstream Road
description: The Emigrant Trail in the pioneer days of California turned to the south at this point for approximately three miles, then went across the summit of the Sierras about a mile south of the present railroad. It was here that the Donner Party missed the trail owing to the early snows, resulting in the tragedy.
address: West side of Cold Stream Rd, 800 ft south of Donner Pass Rd, just past Cold Stream Day Use sign, Truckee

image.alt: Emigrant Trail on Coldstream Road, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Emigrant Trail to Nevada City
description: In 1850, the year after the fabulous gold strike on Deer Creek in Nevada City, the Overland Emigrant Trail branched off the original Bear River route at this point on its way to Nevada City and the Sacramento Valley. Soon thereafter the trail became a toll road and later a public road. The deep vehicle ruts can still be seen as they cross and recross Highway 20 many times.
address: Washington Ridge Vista Point, Hwy 20, 0.2 m. west of Washington Rd

image.alt: Emigrant Trail to Nevada City, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Ernie Bierwagen
description: Ernie was born February 1915 and died in February 2004, in Chicago Park, California. He was close to the land and the people he met along the way. He was a timeless leader in the agricultural community, his church, and was a friend to many. Always genuine, he was a man of strong faith that sustained him and those around him. Ernie and his family devoted their lives to making this world a better place. The love of the community by the Bierwagen Family continues to this day. Ernie often brought his family to observe the construction of this beautiful Reservoir. He knew the importance of water to nature, the food supply and the lives of all people. Ernie served 25 years as a Nevada Irrigation District Division II Director. With passion, he strived to protect and enhance the water supply. To all that visit this beautiful area, please enjoy.
address: Larsen Rd about 1000 ft from the Orchard Springs access road.

image.alt: Ernie Bierwagen

name: Felix Gillet
description: Here lived and worked Felix Gillet, A Frenchman by birth, an American by adoption from 1871 to 1908 through whose vision and perseverance the horticulture of the world was enriched by the propagation and culture of walnuts, filberts, chestnuts, prunes. In commemoration of his work this tablet was set March 7, 1933 by the WomenÃ
address: Stone pillar by driveway at intersection of Nursery and Nihell Sts, Nevada City

image.alt: Felix Gillet, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Unknown authorUnknown author, via Wikimedia Commons (Public domain)

name: Fippin's Blacksmith Shop
description: Republic of Rough and Ready April 1850 - July 1850. This community of 3000 people seceded from the Union to declare a free and independent state. Colonel E. F. Brundage was elected president. Congressional action on California statehood was pending at the time. Built in the mid 1850s by John Fippin and John Single. Fippin married Single's daughter Julia in 1867, and they raised 10 children. Local legend has it that one of Lotta Crabtree's earliest performances was atop the anvil here. The building today is a combination of the original blacksmith shop and Single's carpenter shop. W. H. (Bill) Fippin and Frank A. Fippin followed their father as smiths and ran the business until well into the 20th century. Members of the Fippin and Single families have served Rough and Ready for generations as County officials, School Trustees and Postmasters.

image.alt: Fippin's Blacksmith Shop, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: First Long Distance Telephone
description: Built in 1877, the Ridge Telephone Co., connected French Corral with French Lake 58 miles away. It was operated by the Milton Mining Co., from this building which was built about 1853. This plaque donated by Sadie Winn Brainard. Note: This plaque was originally placed on the brick building in which the telephone office was located. After the building was razed in 1955 it was placed on a concrete monument on the shoulder of the present Pleasant Valley Road in front of a neighboring residence. The 1877 date is an error. The line began operation in 1878. The claim of being the "world's first" is still being debated. However, its challengers did not have the track record of the Ridge Telephone Company, which operated successfully into the 20th century over a much longer distance.
address: In front of private home at 20342 Pleasant Valley Rd, four m. west of Hwy 49

image.alt: First Long Distance Telephone, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: First Motorcycle Across Sierra
description: "George Adams Wyman. The first person to cross America by motorcycle, riding from San Francisco to New York City. May 16–July 6, 1903. George A. Wyman was the first person to make a transcontinental Journey across America by means of a motorized vehicle. Wyman rode a 200cc, 1.25-horsepower 1902 'California' motor-bicycle manufactured by the California Motor Company. On May 20th, Wyman departed the Summit Hotel at Donner Pass. He followed the railroad tracks through the snow sheds to intercept the road near the head of Donner Lake. He continued another 10 miles to Truckee, where he asked for directions and was urged to take the Prosser House road to the Boca siding. At Boca, he switched back to the tracks, passing through Verdi on to Reno, where he stopped for the night."
address: Inside Museum of Truckee History at Amtrak Station, 10065 Donner Pass Rd., Truckee CA

image.alt: First Motorcycle Across Sierra

name: First Transcontinental Railroad--Truckee
description: While construction on Sierra tunnels delayed Central Pacific, advance forces at Truckee began building forty miles of track east and west of Truckee, moving all supplies by wagon and sled. Summit Tunnel was opened in December, 1867. The line reached Truckee April 3, 1868, and the Sierra was conquered. Rails reached Reno June 19, and construction advanced eastward one mile daily toward the meeting with Union Pacific at Promontory May 10,1869, to complete the first transcontinental railroad.
address: Track side of Amtrak station, 10065 Donner Pass Rd

image.alt: First Transcontinental Railroad--Truckee, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Ken Lund from Reno, Nevada, USA, via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 2.0)

name: First Wagons Across the Sierra
description: The Stephens-Townsend-Murphy Party, with guidance from old Caleb Greenwood and Chief Truckee, brought their wagons up the Truckee River bed and on Nov. 25,1844, 191 days out of Council Bluffs, Iowa, passed this point and crossed the mighty Sierra summit directly to the west, blazing the famous California Emigrant Trail for all who followed.
address: West end Donner Lake, corner Donner Pass Rd and S. Shore Dr, west of Truckee

image.alt: First Wagons Across the Sierra, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Gardner, Alexander, 1821-1882, via Wikimedia Commons (Public domain)

name: Five-Stamp Mill 1893
description: Used to crush gold ore at the Fortuna Mine near Nevada City. Ore fed under iron 'stamps' from rear hopper. Steam, water or electric belt-driven power turned wheel which revolved cams. Donated to Nevada City by Bill Magers.

image.alt: Five-Stamp Mill 1893
image.originalAlt: Five-Stamp Mill 1893 in

name: Floriston (Bronco Site)
description: Established in 1872 as Bronco, this railroad wood stop became a noted ice and lumber center and was renamed in 1891. The Floriston Pulp and Paper Company built the present town after 1899 and operated one of the worldÃ
address: Eastbound Floriston offramp on I-80 east of Truckee

image.alt: Floriston (Bronco Site), historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Foote's Crossing Road
description: Foote's Crossing Road was built by A. D. Foote in 1913 as an all-season short-cut from Columbia Hill to the mines of the Alleghany area. Foote was president of the Tightner Mine Co., the largest gold producer in the region at that time. The road, a 15-foot shelf hanging on the sheer canyon wall above the Middle Fork of the Yuba River, is a spectacular engineering feat and scenic wonder. The rock walls, built without mortar are still intact today. Unforeseen difficulties in building the road caused it to far exceed specifications so Foote personally financed 60% of the total cost, the balance being paid by Nevada and Sierra counties and the U.S. Forest Service.
address: From intersection of Cruzon Grade and Tyler-Foote Rds in Nevada County to Alleghany in Sierra County (See Note on access below)

image.alt: Foote's Crossing Road
image.originalAlt: Foote's Crossing Road in

name: Gaston
description: A historic mining community. Placer mining around Gaston began in the early 1850's. The first hard rock mine, the California Mine, was established in 1856. An ample supply of water was ensured by the arrival of the North Bloomfield ditch in 1870. In 1898, the mine, now known as the Gaston Mine, was acquired by the San Francisco based Gaston Gold Mining Company, which substantially upgraded operations. A town blossomed around the mine. The population was recorded as about 200 in 1900. There was stage service to the town of Washington, and to Nevada City. By 1904, the town boasted two stores, a hotel, a saloon, a hardware store, a Post Office, a water system, electric lights, a telephone line to Nevada City, a fire company and a school. The mine closed during World War I, because of a shortage of labor and supplies needed for the war effort. There was intermittent mining after the war. By the 1940s', the last remaining building was in shambles. Today, nothing readily visible remains of Gaston.
address: Roughly midway between Graniteville and town of Washington, near the intersection of Gaston and Hoosier Rds, in the Tahoe National Forest.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Gaston
image.originalAlt: Gaston in

name: Gateway Cabin
description: This cabin was one of several built by Jack Wolert for Dick Joseph in 1939 on US Route 40. The cabin was constructed of lumber salvaged from houses being dismantled five miles north of Truckee at Hobart Mills and served as the Gateway Motel's office. Originally located at the site of the current Gateway Shopping Center on Donner Pass Road (historic US 40). It was donated to the Truckee Donner Historical Society in 1990 and moved to this location on May 23, 1991. Thanks to community efforts, the cabin has been restored and serves as a historical research library.

image.alt: Gateway Cabin, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: George Schaffer
description: One of Truckee's earliest settlers, Schaffer in 1866 built the first lumber mill in the town. He freighted the locomotive 'San Mateo' to Truckee in a winter crossing of the Sierra prior to the laying of Central Pacific tracks. This building 'The Star Hotel' was built by Schaffer probably for his family. When mill operations were relocated up Martis Creek in 1871, this building was converted to a hotel and has remained in continuous operation to this day. For 24 years, the Truckee Basin supplied timber to railroads, mines and boom towns through 'Uncle George's' enterprises. Note: The Schaffer family cemetery, located at 10233 Schaffer Dr., may be visited by the public.

image.alt: George Schaffer, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Gilmore Flying Field
description: Gilmore Flying Field. This windsock marks the site of one of the first flying fields in the United States. Here Lyman Gilmore, a contemporary of the Wright Brothers, pioneered in aviation by designing and building his own planes and gliders as early as 1898. Plaque on monument in front of school dedicated 1968 by Grand Lodge, Free and Accepted Masons: Lyman Gilmore, Jr. 1874-1951. Lyman Gilmore, Jr. established the first commercial airport on this site in 1907. To him and his memory the people of this community dedicate this school as the Lyman Gilmore School. Note: Gilmore did not use this field until the winter of 1911-12. As the Daily Transcript reported on January 29, 1912, "The principal place of business [of the Gilmore Airship Co.] was recently moved from Colfax to Grass Valley, and the meeting on Saturday evening was the first with the present board of directors." This pole was used originally at Gilmore Field, which Gilmore always claimed was the first commercial airport on the West Coast. The CETA mural on the north wall of the multipurpose building was painted in 1978.
address: Lyman Gilmore School, corner Gilmore Way and W. Main St, Grass Valley

image.alt: Gilmore Flying Field, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Glasson House
description: This Victorian/Greek Revival house was built in 1866 for Miles P. O'Connor, who came to California in 1849 and to Nevada County in 1852. He served as justice of the peace for Little York township in 1852-53 and for Grass Valley 1862-69. He served one term in the state assembly and two in the state senate. He earned a fortune from the famous Idaho gold mine and was instrumental in creation of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. After selling this house to John Polglase in 1873, he moved to San Jose and funded various institutions, including a sanitarium later known as O'Connor Hospital. John Polglase, a hard-rock miner from Cornwall, came to Grass Valley in 1864. In 1875 Polglase bought an interest in John Glasson's grocery and provisions store while continuing to work underground in the Idaho mine. In 1882 he sold the house to his partner, and Glasson and his heirs owned it from then until 1973. John Glasson, like Polglase, came here in 1864 from England, and like O'Connor he played a prominent role in Nevada County history. He bought the local gas company in 1885-86 and in 1888 merged it with the Grass Valley Electric Light and Motor Co. He built the countyÃ

image.alt: Glasson House, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Glenbrook Basin
description: Early In the 1850's Glenbrook Basin first became known as the site of county fairs, horse racing and other special events. When Olympia Park, located at the south end of the basin, opened in 1901, Glenbrook Basin became the recreational center of Nevada County and much of Northern California, and remained so for over fifty years. People would come to the basin by horse and buggy or later the automobile, but those wishing for a little more excitement would often use the Nevada County Traction Company's trolley, or the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. The large grandstands of the three-quarter-mile racetrack stood near the junction of present Brunswick Road and Sutton Way. Trotting, pacing, and running horse races were held at the track, and motor car races began in 1913. The racetrack was one of the first electrically lighted racetracks in the state. A dam was constructed just northeast of the existing electrical substation to form Lake Olympia. The lake soon became one of the BasinÃ
address: Sutton Way, south of Brunswick Rd, next to bus shelter in Glenbrook Plaza parking lot

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Glenbrook Basin
image.originalAlt: Glenbrook Basin in

name: Graniteville
description: A camp established in this area in 1850 was called Eureka; then later, Eureka South. By 1856 it sometimes had a winter population of 1000. Rich placer mining deposits were played out by 1865, but quartz mining continued. By 1867, total gold taken from this area was estimated at 20 million dollars. Area also important as distribution point for water from reservoirs, fed to hydraulic mines below by ditches. Name changed to Graniteville when Post Office was established on Aug. 26, 1867.
address: No. Bloomfield-Graniteville Rd, across from house at No. 35177. 50 ft past gazebo bearing Graniteville sign.

image.alt: Graniteville, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Grass Valley AME Church and School Site
description: "Across the street, on lots 315, 317, 319 and 323 S. Church Street, the Grass Valley African Methodist Episcopal Church and School were erected in 1854 and 1865 respectively by the local African American community. Reverend Peter Green and Church trustee and former slave Isaac Sanks, along with the other members of the congregation, worked to abolish slavery, secure voting rights for African American men, eliminate the ban on testimony of African Americans in court, and gain access to public accommodations and education. The AME Church was known for its exceptional choir and accomplished musicians. It was the site for fundraising events, literary readings, and the celebration of the Emancipation Proclamation. African American parents, whose children were denied entry into white schools, built their own schoolhouse next to the Church. Both the Church and School were sold in 1893 at public auction to Charles Clinch and the funds returned to the AME Church. Shortly thereafter, the new owners built the four Victorian homes that still stand on the property today."
address: 315-323 S. Church Street, Grass Valley. Plaque located across the street in front of 328 S. Church St.

image.alt: Grass Valley AME Church and School Site

name: Hard Rock Gold Mining
description: Hard Rock Gold Mining 1850-1957. Within a one-mile radius of this spot more than 500 million dollars worth of gold was produced by deep mines, including the Empire, North Star and the Idaho-Maryland. During that time this was the worldÃ
address: Memorial Park, Grass Valley, near creek by Race St culvert

image.alt: Hard Rock Gold Mining, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Joseph Nguyen, G Tour Photos, via Google

name: Hartung House
description: In August of 1888 Nevada and Emma (Schwartz) Hartung purchased about 220 acres bordering Pleasant Valley Rd. and Piper Lane in Pleasant Valley (now Penn Valley). At the time of purchase, a small house and a barn were on the property. These two structures likely were built in the early 1860s when the property was owned by James and Hannah Saxon. The first house, still standing in the 1930's, had deteriorated and was razed. The well, located in the front porch of the house remains. The barn now extended on both ends, still exists. The Hartungs built the current house in 1889. Pear, apple and walnut crops became the primary means of support for the family. A few of the original pear trees remain. Nevada August Hartung was born in August 1853 on the shores of Webber Lake, California in a wagon train on the family's way west from Illinois. It is reported that Nevada's given name was the result of the parents' awe at their first sight of the Sierra Nevada Mountains. He grew up on his father Adolph Hartung's farm at Kentucky Flat and married Emma Schwartz in 1880. By the time of their 1888 purchase of the property, the Hartungs had three children, Frank (b.1880), Herman (b. 1884) and Mamie (b. 1886). The Hartungs bought the farm from Samuel and Agnes Yeo (owners 1888-1888) and prior to that the property was owned by Joseph Peers (1886-1888) and the Saxons (ca.1860-1886). In 1849-1850, John Rose and William Reynolds operated a trading post and cattle corral, both thought to be located on what became the Hartung property. The current owner, Pete Brennan, acquired the property in 1971 and operates a horse boarding facility.

image.alt: Hartung House, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Hobart Mills Site
description: First plaque dedicated 1938 by descendants of Walter Scott Hobart is about 0.2 m. north of the junction of Hobart Mills Rd with Old Hwy 89 on west side of Old 89: This monument marks the townsite of Hobart Mills, California. A logging and lumbering enterprise of sixty-six years life in the Sierra Nevada mountains it commemorates and honors its founder Walter Scott Hobart 1839-1892 and those whose loyalty, industry and ability made its successful operation possible.
address: Just east of Hwy 89, five miles north of Truckee. There are two plaques, about 200 yards apart

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Hobart Mills Site
image.originalAlt: Hobart Mills Site in

name: Hobart Mills Site Second Plaque
description: Walter Scott Hobart's Sierra Nevada Wood & Lumber Company moved machinery, buildings and railroad equipment here from Lake Tahoe in 1896. The town and business flourished for 40 years, ceasing operations in 1936. Fibreboard Corporation operated a mill and railroad here from 1946 to 1955, logging timber purchased from Southern Pacific. The old mill was burned in 1964. Hobart Mills was an important lumbering center and a major contributor to the heritage of the Eastern Sierra.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Hobart Mills Site Second Plaque
image.originalAlt: Hobart Mills Site Second Plaque in

name: Hooligan Rock
description: Behind this broken rock, vigilante justice in the form of a tar and feather party or other form of physical punishment was meted out to the villains and ruffians who plagued Truckee through the dawn of the 20th-century. Constable Jacob Teeter bravely fought against vigilantism and his friend, attorney C. F. McGlashan, blasted the rock, ending its symbolism of terror, and used parts of the rock in a wall around his mansion. His granddaughter, Nona, recalled that the rock formed a 'backdrop of ugly secret scenes, floggings in the night, scalding tar and a smother of feathers, curses and screams for mercy.
address: 11200 Donner Pass Rd, east end of parking lot

image.alt: Hooligan Rock, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Gold Crush Climbing Gym, via Google

name: Humbug/North Blomfield Cemetery
description: "This 'hallowed place', that resides with in the boundaries of the Malakoff State Historical Park represents a 'tapestry of humanity.' Those who dared to carve out a place for themselves in an unknown and untamed wilderness. They came seeking riches, changing the landscape they encountered for all of time. They left behind a legacy entwined with turmoil, destruction, invention and lives lived hard. In 1855, Isreal Joiner, who was shot for stealing gold from a 'sluice box', and George Carter who lived to be 80, were the first to be buried in Humbug Cemetery. In 1857 when the town got its post office, the name was changed to North Bloomfield. George Edwards, John Enders, John McNulty, and Louise Miller; they were the first to be buried under the new banner, North Bloomfield Cemetery. By the end of the Nineteenth Century, as the mining played out, this town was all but dead. However, in an almost bazaar [sic] manner this cemetery has continued to grow! As of 1984 there were 170 recorded buried here from Austria, Australia, Bavaria, Canada, China, Denmark, England, France, Germany, Holland, Ireland, Portugal, Prussia, Scotland and America. In March 2017, Debra Phanner, a long time Park asst. was buried here. So, as you enter this portal into the past know that you are walking on 'hallowed ground' with many untold stories waiting to be discovered!"
address: North Bloomfield Rd, west of North Bloomfield, Malakoff Diggins State Historical Park

image.alt: Humbug/North Blomfield Cemetery

name: Jacob van Blaren
description: Anthony House Way Station, Pub & Tavern was located just off shore from Commodore Park. It was a stage stop between Marysville & the Virginia City Silver Mines. Jacob Van Blaren, a Civil War bugler, stopped at Anthony House for a period of time and died while staying there. He was in his forties at his death. Proprietors of Anthony House decided to bury him behind the building and it wasn't until Boise Cascade was in negotiations with the historical society that they named the area surrounding his burial site Bugler Park. It is uncertain at what point in Lake Wildwood's history that the park's name was changed to Commodore Park

On July 26, 1995 the diner arrived in Truckee, California. No other diner of it's (sic) size and class had ever been trucked across the continent. After extensive restoration the diner was re-opened to the public on December 7, 1995.

name: Kohler Building
description: Built by Henry Kohler, pioneer merchant and miner born in Germany in 1825. He came to Washington from Indiana in 1854. He mined until 1861 when he opened a general merchandise store to supply groceries, hardware and mining supplies to the area.

name: Kruger-White House
description: Designated a National Historic Landmark 1982: This property Kruger-White House has been placed on the National Register of Historic Places by the United States Department of the Interior. Note: W. H. Kruger, a lumberman and entrepreneur, built this impressive home 107 years ago. Mr. Kruger contributed significantly to the 19th century community development of Truckee as one of the town's leading commercial pioneers. Mr. C. B. White, a banker, bought the house in 1904. Today, the Kruger-White home stands as an historic architectural landmark in a town which, for over a century, has represented an important gateway to California.

name: Lake Tahoe Railway & Transportation Company Site
description: The Lake Tahoe Railway & Transportation Company was incorporated on December 1898 by Duane Leroy Bliss. The Narrow Gauge Railroad opened for seasonal traffic on May 1, 1900. The line ran from the Southern Pacific Depot in Truckee, 15 miles south to the shores of Lake Tahoe. Tahoe City hosted the railroad's shops. The Tahoe Tavern hotel and pier where the train met the Bliss steamer - Tahoe. Southern Pacific Purchased the line and converted it to standard gauge in 1926. Passenger Service operated until abandonment in November 1943.
address: Just east of 10130 W. River St, across the railroad tracks from Amtrak station. The plaque is missing. If you have any information about its whereabouts, please contact the publisher.

name: McGlashan Point
description: Dedicated August 10,1986 in honor of Charles Fayette McGlashan 1847-1931. Truckee's Patriarch, Historian, Author, Editor, Attorney, Legislator, Inventor, Entomologist and Astronomer. His last public address was given in 1926 at the Donner Summit Bridge Dedication. This site is named in his memory by the people of Truckee.
address: Overlook next to Donner Summit Bridge on Donner Pass Rd, Old Hwy 40

image.alt: McGlashan Point, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: McGuashen Butterfly Collection *
description: "Charles Fayette McGlashan, noted author of History of the Donner Party: A Tragedy of the Sierra, started his butterfly collection in 1872. In the early 1900s, his daughter, Ximena, joined him in collecting and arranging the Lepidoptera collection. Ximena, then referred to as The Butterfly Princess, was instrumental in growing the collection and started her own business, The Butterfly Farm. She continued this venture until she attended college. With her father, she also wrote and published The Butterfly Farmer, a monthly periodical. Their collection included up to 20,000 specimens, and they were granted a number of entomological mounting patents. First housed in the original Rocking Stone Tower, the collection was moved several times and finally moved here in 2015."

name: Memorial Arch
description: First plaque: "THE ARCH Erected on the entrance of the old Masonic building in 1909. Destroyed by a gas explosion in 1993. Dedicated to the Town of Truckee - October 27, 2007 by Truckee Lodge #200 F&AM Gary E. Miner, W.M. Dedicated to Truth, Equality & Freedom." Second plaque: "Celebrating 150 years of Freemasons in Truckee. Lodge #200 established on October 14, 1869. Dedicated to the Town of Truckee October 14, 2019 by Truckee Lodge #200 F&AM Christopher Rochefort, W.M. Dedicated to Truth, Equality & Freedom." Note: The Arch is all that remains of the historic Masonic Lodge # 200 building, built in 1909 and destroyed by fire and explosion in 1993. It reportedly was the only lodge to have had a chiseled, Sierra granite entrance-way, which was preserved after the fire. In 2007, the entrance-way Arch was reconstructed and placed on the grounds of the Truckee-Donner Historical Society's Old Jail Museum.

The land and swimming pool were donated by the Empire Mine Co.; the club house was donated by the Idaho Maryland Mine Co.; and people of the area subscribed $8,000. It now contains five plaques honoring those who served our country in the armed forces.

name: Mt. Elisha Stephens
description: Stephens-Townsend-Murphy Party of 1844 First Pioneer Wagons Over the Sierra Nevada. On May 22, 1844, this small wagon party of 50 men, women and children 'jumped off' from Council Bluffs, Iowa, bound for California. These courageous pioneers were the first emigrants to take wagons over the Sierra Nevada, opening the Truckee Route of the California Trail. The Murphy, Townsend, Miller, and Hitchcock families formed the main groups of the party. Caleb Greenwood was the hired guide, and Elisha Stephens was elected Captain of the wagon train. After following the Oregon Trail to Fort Hall, Idaho, they turned away from the well-worn path and headed west into the unfamiliar Nevada desert. A Paiute Indian Chief showed them how to reach a river flowing from the Sierra Nevada, which they named 'Truckee' in his honor. Overcoming severe conditions, they followed the Truckee River to the base of the Sierra. Six members, on horseback, continued to follow the Truckee River and eventually arrived safely at Sutter's New Helvetia on December 10, 1844. Six of the wagons were left in the charge of two men, and young Moses Schallenberger, near Truckee Lake (present Donner Lake). Near this spot, with two feet of snow on the ground, they worked the remaining five wagons across the summit of the mighty Sierra Nevada on November 25, 1844. Deepening snow forced them to camp near Big Bend, on the South Yuba River. Leaving the wagons with two men and the women and children, the remaining 17 men continued on to New Helvetia to seek help. Somehow, John Sutter persuaded the men to enlist in his forces taking part in a Mexican civil war. Eventually the families were reunited, and all members (including two babies born on the way) arrived safely at Sutter's Fort in early March 1845. Note: Mount Elisha Stephens is west of bridge on north side of road.
address: Parking lot next to Donner Summit Bridge on Donner Pass Rd, Old Hwy 40

name: Mt. Saint Mary's Convent and Academy
description: Mt. St. Mary's School. To this spot Father Thomas J. Dalton brought the Sisters of Mercy in 1863. The Sisters of Mercy have taught here since that date. Landmark registered and second plaque dedicated 1972 Built by the Reverend Thomas J. Dalton and dedicated May 2, 1865 by Bishop Eugene O'Connell as Sacred Heart Convent and Holy Angels Orphanage. Under the Sisters of Mercy it served from 1866 to 1932 as the first orphanage of the Northern Mines. It functioned as an academy (1868-1965) and a convent (1866-1968).

name: North San Juan Methodist Episcopal Church
description: Erected in 1857 at a cost of $1500, the structure Is the earliest existing church in the county. It contains early furnishings. Second plaque dedicated 1980 by the church board and Chap 10 E Clampus Vitus: North San Juan United Methodist Church 1856. One of the oldest of its faith in continuous operation in California. Escaped three major fires which destroyed the town. The Rev. H. B. Sheldon, first assigned pastor, rode circuit from Brandy City to French Corral on horseback.
address: End of Flume St off Hwy 49

image.alt: North San Juan Methodist Episcopal Church, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: North San Juan Protestant Cemetery
description: The North San Juan Protestant Cemetery is an unusually well preserved and beautiful cemetery and has served as a place of interment since 1851. It holds the remains of hundreds of individuals, with many life stories related to the Gold Rush. The grave monuments within its boundary are exceptionally varied in design and represent fine examples of gravestone art and symbolism. Situated in a lovely tree covered setting, it is one of the best examples of nineteenth century burial customs and history.

image.alt: North San Juan Protestant Cemetery in North San Juan

name: Old Auburn Road Bridge (Site)
description: The Old Auburn Bridge was registered as a landmark in 1996 by Nevada County. The bridge consisted of a single span, pin-connected steel pony truss, 66-feet in length, 11-feet in width, with a timber deck carrying a single traffic lane. It was built in 1897 by Cotton Brothers and Co. of Oakland, California. In 2004, the old bridge was dismantled by the County as unsafe and replaced by a modern span. Recognizing the historical significance of the spot, the County erected a monument containing a bronze plaque: Historic Marker Perrin Crossing. This bridge replaces the original steel truss span constructed in 1897 and remained in continuous service until 2004. This crossing received its name from Joseph and Susan Perrin, who settled nearby in 1851. Close to this crossing the Perrin family operated a stagecoach stop known for its hospitality. This road was part of the original stagecoach route between Sacramento and Grass Valley. Note: Remains of the stagecoach stop can be seen about 100 ft. south of the monument
address: Old Auburn Rd, southeast of modern bridge over Wolf Creek about 4 m. south of McCourtney Rd

image.alt: Old Auburn Road Bridge (Site), historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Old Bear River Bridge
description: Since 1852 bridges near this site have served as the gateway to western Nevada County. This 1924 span was the first concrete bridge at this site and features a Roman style single arch design, constructed in a continuous pour method. The Colfax Highway Association led a citizens group that saved the bridge from the wrecker's ball in 1986, and placed this monument in 1992.
address: Hwy 174 at Bear River, between Colfax and Chicago Park

image.alt: Old Bear River Bridge, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Old Capitol Building
description: The Old Capitol. Erected in 1870. In its heyday it was the social center of Truckee . . . Many of the leading thespians . . . politicians . . . and other celebrities of that era gathered here . . . Note: This plaque has disappeared. A plaque placed on the front of building by unknown persons reads: Capitol Building 1870 Truckee California. The first brick building in Truckee, it was originally Hurd's Saloon, then the Capitol Saloon, later used by the Piper Opera Company and the County Court.

image.alt: Old Capitol Building, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Old Chinese Herb Shop
description: Old Chinatown. Construction of the Central Pacific Railroad over Donner Summit brought thousands of Chinese laborers here in 1868. They used â€˜squatter's rights' to build a ramshackle colony along the Truckee River on what is now River Avenue East. A huge fire destroyed Chinatown on May 29, 1875, but the Orientals rebuilt their community. Finally, on Nov. 18, 1878, a group of about 500 citizens, under the leadership of the Caucasian League, tore down the shanty town and ordered the 1000 or more Chinese to leave. They simply moved across the river, adjoining this former site of a Chinese herb shop, and built anew. A third fire, on Jan. 3, 1883, consumed the 'new' Chinatown and brought about the gradual end to the Chinese colony here. Bronze plaque dedicated 1980 by Chap 3691 E Clampus Vitus. Text of bronze plaque: Old Chinese Herb Shop.
address: Corner of S.E. River St. and Brockway Rd.

image.alt: Old Chinese Herb Shop, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Old North San Juan School
description: The first school building on this site was moved from Flume Street in 1869. It burned in 1924, and this building was moved here from Lake Vera. This site operated as a school from 1869 until 1984. It is currently used as a community center and library.

name: Old Pacific Fruit Packing Shed
description: A smaller shed, built here in 1926, collapsed during a snowstorm in 1929. Its successor was built in 1930 by the Civilian Conservation Corps. The Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad tracks, depot, and water tower were alongside this building, where peaches and pears from the surrounding orchards were packed and sent to Colfax by train. There the boxes were transferred to standard sized refrigerator freight cars to be shipped to the East.

name: Old Stone Garage
description: Truckee's Old Stone Garage and Site of Town's First Dwelling. In 1863, Joseph Gray built Truckee's first structure on this site for use as the Dutch Flat-Donner Lake Wagon Road's toll station. The old log structure was moved in 1907 to the Englehart property on Church Street, one block east of this site, where it still stands and the Brethren of ECV dedicated a plaque in 1965. In 1909, D. J. Smith, with the help of European-trained stone masons, constructed the present building for use as a livery and garage.

name: Original Ott Assay Office Site
description: Site of the Original Ott Assay Office. J. J. Ott's assay in 1859 helped develop the great Comstock Lode and the State of Nevada. Note: Ott's original Assay Office was built of wood. It burned in the fire of 1863 and Ott moved into the existing brick building next door.
